one_liner: A 58-year-old man presents with 8 months of progressive weakness and wasting of his right hand, frequent muscle twitches, and more recently slurred speech and difficulty swallowing.

stage2.hpi_extension: He first noticed weakness and cramping in his right hand about 8 months ago, which made it hard to button his shirts and open jars. Over time he developed visible muscle twitches (fasciculations) in his right forearm, shoulder, and tongue, and his right hand has become noticeably thinner (atrophic), especially between the thumb and index finger. In the last 2 months he has developed slurred speech, drooling, and choking on thin liquids. He denies any sensory symptoms, pain, bowel or bladder dysfunction, or cognitive change. He has fallen twice from tripping on his right foot.
stage2.family_history: No known neurologic disease; paternal grandfather died 'of a muscle disease' in his 60s
stage2.social_history: Retired electrician. Former smoker (quit 10 years ago). Social alcohol.
stage2.physical_exam: Mental status normal. Tongue: atrophy and coarse fasciculations, dysarthric speech. Motor: marked wasting of the right first dorsal interosseous and thenar eminence, 3/5 strength in right intrinsic hand muscles, 4/5 in right biceps and triceps, 4+/5 in right hip flexion; visible fasciculations over right arm and both thighs. Tone increased (spastic) in the right lower extremity. Reflexes: 3+ at the right biceps, triceps, patella, and Achilles; positive Babinski on the right; brisk jaw jerk. Sensation is completely intact to light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception in all distributions. Bowel and bladder function preserved.

lab_results.result: Nerve conduction study / EMG: nerve conduction velocities and sensory responses normal. EMG shows widespread fibrillations, positive sharp waves, fasciculations, and large-amplitude long-duration motor unit potentials with reduced recruitment in muscles of three or more spinal regions (cervical, thoracic, lumbosacral, bulbar), consistent with active and chronic denervation with reinnervation.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Widespread active and chronic denervation with preserved sensory nerve conduction in multiple body regions is the electrodiagnostic hallmark of ALS and is required to meet El Escorial / Awaji diagnostic criteria.

lab_results.result: MRI of the brain and cervical spine: no structural lesion, no cord compression, no demyelinating plaques. Mild nonspecific T2 hyperintensity along the corticospinal tracts.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Imaging is obtained to exclude mimics such as cervical spondylotic myelopathy, multiple sclerosis, and structural lesions of the brainstem or cord.

lab_results.result: Sodium 140, glucose 96, creatinine 0.9, calcium 9.5, TSH 2.1. CK mildly elevated at 450 U/L. B12 normal.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Normal metabolic and thyroid studies and normal B12 help exclude metabolic/nutritional mimics. Mild CK elevation is common in ALS from denervation.

stage5.final_clue: The patient is started on riluzole (glutamate antagonist) and referred to a multidisciplinary ALS clinic for speech therapy, nutrition, PT/OT, and early discussion of non-invasive ventilation. He is counseled about the progressive nature of the disease and typical 3-5 year survival.

debrief.classic_presentation: ALS classically presents in middle-aged to older adults (50-60 years) with progressive, asymmetric, painless weakness and a distinctive combination of upper motor neuron (UMN) and lower motor neuron (LMN) signs in the same limb or body region, with no sensory deficits, no bowel/bladder dysfunction, and typically no cognitive impairment. LMN signs: weakness, atrophy, fasciculations, hyporeflexia. UMN signs: spasticity, hyperreflexia, pathologic reflexes (Babinski, Hoffmann), pseudobulbar affect, and brisk jaw jerk. Bulbar-onset disease (dysarthria, dysphagia, tongue atrophy and fasciculations) carries the worst prognosis. Median survival is 3-5 years; death is usually from respiratory failure (diaphragmatic weakness).
debrief.pathophysiology: ALS is a neurodegenerative disease of both upper motor neurons (Betz cells and corticospinal tracts) and lower motor neurons (anterior horn cells of the spinal cord and brainstem motor nuclei). Most cases are sporadic; ~10% are familial, most famously due to mutations in superoxide dismutase 1 (SOD1) (gain-of-function/oxidative injury), C9orf72 (hexanucleotide repeat, also causes FTD), TDP-43, and FUS. Mechanisms include glutamate excitotoxicity, oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, and protein aggregation. Sensory pathways and autonomic function are spared — a key distinguishing feature.
debrief.treatment_overview: No curative therapy exists. Riluzole — a glutamate release inhibitor — prolongs survival by approximately 3 months. Edaravone (free-radical scavenger) slows functional decline in selected patients. Supportive care is central: multidisciplinary ALS clinic, non-invasive ventilation (NIV/BiPAP) for respiratory insufficiency (improves survival and quality of life), PEG tube for nutrition when dysphagia compromises caloric intake, speech therapy and communication devices, and early advance-care planning. Baclofen or tizanidine for spasticity; dextromethorphan/quinidine for pseudobulbar affect.
high_yield_pearls: ALS = mixed UMN + LMN signs, NO sensory deficits, and NO bowel/bladder involvement.
LMN signs (atrophy, fasciculations, weakness) + UMN signs (hyperreflexia, spasticity, Babinski) in the same limb is the hallmark.
Anterior horn cell degeneration underlies the LMN signs; lateral corticospinal tract degeneration underlies the UMN signs.
Familial ALS is most classically associated with superoxide dismutase 1 (SOD1) mutations (zinc/copper-dependent).
Riluzole (glutamate antagonist) is the only disease-modifying drug that prolongs survival; death is usually from respiratory failure.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Neurology and Special Senses chapter, 'Spinal cord and related lesions — ALS' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Neurology and Special Senses chapter, 'Degenerative diseases — Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis' section.
